summary: Gruber responds to a New York Times investigation suggesting Apple engaged in anti-competitive behavior by ranking its own apps atop App Store search results. While acknowledging the investigation's merit, he argues the evidence better supports incompetence than malice. He highlights developer David Barnard's observation that App Store search has been broken since 2008, noting that the Times's own data shows absurd results like the Compass app ranking second for 'podcast' searches. Gruber concludes that Apple's broken search is more embarrassing than deliberate manipulation would be, and hopes Apple will treat its search incompetence as seriously as it treats antitrust accusations.
key_points: The NYT found Apple's own apps dominated App Store search results even when less relevant than competitors, and Apple acknowledged the issue and adjusted its algorithm
Developer David Barnard argues the results are better explained by App Store search being fundamentally broken since 2008 rather than deliberate anti-competitive behavior
The NYT's own data undermines the malice theory: a 'podcast' search returned Compass and Tips before third-party podcast apps, which serves no competitive purpose
Apple making both Schiller and Cue available to the Times signals how seriously they take antitrust accusations, but they should take search quality equally seriously
The parallel to Google antitrust investigations is noted but Gruber sees a key distinction between intentional manipulation and systemic incompetence
money_quotes: Burying third-party podcast apps 15 results down isn't anticompetitive, it's stupid.
No one — no one searching for 'podcast' wants the Compass app, yet it's ranked second.

The fact that Apple made both Schiller and Cue available to The Times shows how seriously they take accusations of anti-competitive behavior. Let's hope they start taking accusations of incompetent stewardship of App Store search just as seriously.

Apple might not be lying about manipulating search themselves, but it's 100X more embarrassing that they have allowed one of the most important experiences on their $50B/yr platform to be so broken and easy to manipulate for more than a decade.
Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by stupidity.
App Store search has been an unmitigated disaster since the App Store was released in 2008.
key_insight: Apple's App Store search results favoring its own apps are better explained by a decade of neglected, broken search infrastructure than by deliberate anti-competitive manipulation — but that incompetence is arguably more damning.
